name: Poem: "Life" — Henry Van Dyke

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 68 Q113 (real quoted line, with answer key — found while researching Day 3's Teamwork/Courage topic, stashed and used here)
civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated MCQ set, figures of speech per line

researchNotes: One real confirmed line (stashed from Day-3 research, when this poem's authorship was tested but not yet authored) plus a 6-question web-sourced set. This poem's figure-of-speech labelling leans on some less-common devices already seen rarely in this syllabus (Litotes, Symbolism) — worth extra attention.

reading.intro: A reflective poem about living life with courage, purpose, and without regret or fear of the future. Real exam evidence confirms a specific quoted line's authorship; web research fills in the figure-of-speech detail, including two devices (Litotes, Symbolism) not seen elsewhere in this syllabus.

qa.question: Real exam: "Still seeking what I sought when but a boy, new friendship, high adventure and a crown." Who wrote this?
qa.answer: Henry Van Dyke, in "Life" — confirmed via a real TNPSC question testing this exact line's authorship against three other poet options.

qa.question: What is the poem's central theme?
qa.answer: Living life forward-facing, with courage and clear purpose — neither rushing nor dragging, not mourning losses, and not holding back out of fear of an unknown future.

qa.question: Who was Henry Van Dyke?
qa.answer: An American author (1852-1933) — earned degrees from Princeton, served over 20 years as a Presbyterian minister, then returned to Princeton as a professor of English for nearly 20 years.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Let me but live my life from year to year."
qa.answer: Repetition (the phrase structure repeats a forward-moving, cyclical idea) — cited as Repetition rather than the other options in a real-style question.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Not mourning for the things that disappear."
qa.answer: Litotes — an understatement that affirms something by negating its opposite (saying 'not mourning' to actually emphasize calm acceptance, rather than stating the acceptance directly).

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "So let the way wind up the hill or down."
qa.answer: Personification — 'the way' (the path of life) is treated as something that can actively 'wind', given an active, almost willful quality.

qa.question: Which phrase in the poem is the clearest example of Symbolism?
qa.answer: "The road's last turn" — symbolizing the end of life/journey, rather than a literal physical road.

questions.prompt: "Still seeking what I sought when but a boy, new friendship, high adventure and a crown" was written by:
questions.explanation: Henry Van Dyke — confirmed via a real TNPSC question.

questions.prompt: "Let me but live my life from year to year" is cited as an example of:
"Not mourning for the things that disappear" is an example of:
"So let the way wind up the hill or down" uses which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Personification — the 'way' given the active quality of winding.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is the clearest example of Symbolism in the poem?
questions.explanation: "The road's last turn" symbolizes life's end.

questions.prompt: Henry Van Dyke, in addition to being a poet, spent over 20 years working as a:
questions.explanation: A Presbyterian minister, before returning to Princeton as an English professor.
